pair_convention.spot_explanation: SGD/JPY at 122.00 means 1 SGD = 122.00 JPY. The pair is derived from USD/SGD 1.2710 and USD/JPY 157.63. A rising SGD/JPY pair means SGD is strengthening versus JPY — it takes more yen to buy one Singapore dollar. A falling SGD/JPY pair means SGD is weakening versus JPY, or equivalently JPY is strengthening against SGD — it takes fewer yen to buy one Singapore dollar.
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (SGD weakens vs JPY, JPY strengthens vs SGD)

executive_summary: SGD/JPY trades at 122.00, representing a 1 SGD = 122 JPY cross derived from USD/SGD 1.2710 and USD/JPY 157.63. The pair has appreciated approximately 11.3% over the past twelve months as SGD structural strength has compounded against accumulated JPY weakness, and has risen roughly 0.5% over the past month as the Iran-driven safe-haven JPY bid (typically SGD/JPY-bearish) was partially offset by SGD's own resilience as Asia's dominant financial sanctuary. The dominant bilateral narrative is a collision between two of the most structurally compelling currency stories in Asia: a Singapore economy firing on all cylinders with an 18% of GDP current account surplus and a near-certain managed appreciation regime on one side, and a Japanese yen at historically unprecedented undervaluation with a BoJ embarking on a uniquely hawkish normalisation path in G10 on the other. The bilateral factor that diverges most from either standalone dossier is the carry and risk-appetite nexus: SGD/JPY is one of the most compact carry crosses in G10-adjacent space (SORA–BoJ differential of only +7bps), meaning this pair is NOT a traditional carry trade but is instead driven almost entirely by the relative pace of two diverging policy regimes and their interaction with the Iran geopolitical overlay. The tactical conclusion is that SGD/JPY is in active two-way conflict: the structural SGD appreciation slope has historically dominated, but BoJ fiscal year-end repatriation flows, a hawkish Shunto wage print due imminently, and the BoJ's unique hiking trajectory create credible headwinds that make this the most genuinely contested cross in Singapore's FX universe. Our headline stance is Tactically Neutral, Strategically Bearish SGD/JPY (pair falling) at conviction 2/5 tactically and 3/5 strategically, reflecting that the convergence of two bullish standalone dossiers creates a genuine tug of war, with the BoJ normalisation thesis ultimately winning out over the 1–6 month horizon.

market_context.valuation_assessment: From a PPP perspective, the SGD dossier estimates fair value SGD/JPY at approximately ¥111 (SGD overvalued vs JPY by ~10% on PPP), while the JPY dossier estimates fair value at approximately ¥93 on an OECD PPP basis (JPY undervalued vs USD by 40%; vs SGD the PPP deviation is ~21% indicating JPY is cheap). The midpoint of these PPP estimates implies fair value around ¥100–111, meaning SGD/JPY at 122 is trading approximately 10–22% above PPP fair value — a wide band that is economically meaningful. However, the SGD's managed appreciation regime and Singapore's structural trade surplus historically sustain persistent real overvaluation relative to PPP (BIS REER at the 92nd percentile), while JPY's all-time REER low (0th percentile, 67.73) argues for eventual JPY mean-reversion. The current SGD/JPY spot therefore embeds a 'double overvaluation' premium: SGD is rich, JPY is cheap. This dual PPP signal is the strongest structural reason for medium-term SGD/JPY downside.

market_context.recent_regime: SGD/JPY has traded in a relatively narrow band of approximately ¥118–123 over the past three months, consistent with two powerful forces creating a gravitational equilibrium. The dominant bilateral regime is one of high structural tension: the long-term carry trade community that funded JPY to buy higher-yielding EM assets is under increasing pressure, while Singapore's structural capital inflows and managed appreciation provide a persistent SGD bid that caps any SGD/JPY downside.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: SGD/JPY is NOT a carry trade. The +7bps differential is economically irrelevant — this is a pure directional pair driven by the relative speed of MAS appreciation vs BoJ tightening. The pair's direction is determined almost entirely by relative policy regime strength, capital flow differentials, and cross-asset correlations (particularly equity vol via the safe-haven channel). Unlike AUD/JPY (+310bps) or GBP/JPY (+300bps), SGD/JPY offers no carry compensation for directional risk — all return must come from spot movement. The near-zero carry means the cost of holding a directional position is minimal in interest rate terms. The primary cost is the bid-ask spread and execution in a thin market. This creates a slightly different expression calculus than carry-laden JPY crosses. Critically, if BoJ reaches 1.00% while SORA remains at 0.82%, the bilateral carry flips negative (-18bps), marking the first carry inversion in this cycle and creating a structural headwind for SGD/JPY longs that consensus models appear to underweight.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The SGD/JPY rate differential is the most unusual in the G10+SGD universe: SORA at 0.82% vs BoJ at 0.75% produces a bilateral spread of only +7bps in SGD's favour — effectively parity. This makes SGD/JPY one of the only non-carry-driven JPY crosses in the global FX universe. The tactical rate signal is therefore near-neutral on the carry channel, but the policy regime signals diverge importantly. MAS is in a managed appreciation regime where the S$NEER band slope embeds approximately 2% per annum of implicit SGD appreciation, creating a structural directional bias regardless of the cash rate. The BoJ is hiking — having moved from -0.10% to 0.75% in 23 months — but the March 18 meeting is overwhelmingly expected to produce a hold (~95% probability). Neither central bank will provide a directional shock at the March meeting, creating a bilateral standoff on this factor tactically. The bilateral rate differential including the forward premium is approximately zero, confirming no carry incentive exists in either direction. The key question over 2–4 weeks is whether the Fed's dovish forward guidance (following the -92K February NFP) compresses USD-denominated yields enough to influence SGD's relative position — but this affects USD/SGD more than SGD/JPY directly.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing both central banks on hold in March and the divergence in their respective paths (MAS either tightening or holding with appreciation bias; BoJ hiking later). The near-zero carry means no directional carry bias is priced into forwards — all of SGD/JPY's expected move must come from spot. This makes the pair particularly sensitive to data surprises from either central bank.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic rate picture creates a critical asymmetry: the BoJ is the only G10 central bank hiking, while MAS is either holding or tightening marginally. The Federal Reserve is expected to cut rates by another 50 basis points while the Bank of Japan is expected to hike by the same amount — this creates a convergence dynamic that narrows the USD-JPY differential while MAS's managed float remains anchored. Crucially for SGD/JPY specifically, the BoJ hiking from 0.75% toward 1.00–1.25% (a move of 25–50bps) does NOT widen the bilateral SGD/JPY rate differential appreciably since SORA sits at 0.82% — it actually compresses it from SGD's side to near-zero or even slightly negative. If BoJ reaches 1.00% and SORA remains 0.82%, SGD becomes a negative carry currency vs JPY for the first time in the current cycle. This regime shift — from minimal positive SGD carry to negative carry — is a structural driver for SGD/JPY downside that consensus forecasts appear to underweight. The SGS-JGB 10Y spread is already -18bps negative (JGB yields higher than SGS), meaning Japan's bond market already pays more than Singapore's at the long end, creating an incentive for repatriation flows back to Japan. The interest rate gap between Japan and the United States narrows through 2026, but for SGD/JPY the relevant compression is the bilateral gap, which is already at 7bps and trending toward inversion.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both economies are growing above potential and have inflation near target, but the profiles are mirror images. Singapore's growth differential is extraordinary (6.9% YoY GDP vs Japan's 1.1% YoY) but Singapore's inflation is contained (1.0% MAS Core), while Japan's growth is modest but wage inflation is surging (3.0% base pay, +3.0% labour cash earnings — strongest since 1992). The bilateral growth differential of approximately +5.8 percentage points strongly favours SGD on raw GDP metrics. However, GDP growth rate differentials matter for FX through two channels: attracting capital inflows (bullish SGD/JPY) and influencing central bank policy (bullish JPY if BoJ is forced to hike faster). The imminent Shunto concentrated response day (~March 12) is the key near-term catalyst. If Japanese wage growth confirms 5.5%+ outcomes, the BoJ's normalisation timeline accelerates — directly negative for SGD/JPY. Conversely, Singapore's NODX February print (March 17 release) will reinforce the semiconductor cycle story if it confirms the January +56.1% electronics trajectory. The two data events roughly offset each other in the 2–4 week window, producing a neutral bilateral assessment.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Singapore's growth outperformance will likely persist through H1 2026 as the semiconductor supercycle maintains momentum (WSTS 2026 forecast: $975.5bn, +26.3% YoY). However, the strategic inflation dynamics favour JPY more than SGD because Japan's wage-price cycle is evolving toward self-sustainability for the first time since the 1990s. The Shunto outcome will likely be 5.5%+ per the Rengo demand of 5.85–5.94%, which locks in 3%+ real wage growth and validates the BoJ's hiking path. Singapore's 1.0% core inflation, while ideal for MAS credibility, provides less impetus for aggressive MAS tightening than Japan's 2.6% core-core CPI provides for BoJ action. The relative 'policy surprise' potential is therefore more skewed to JPY strength than SGD strength: if Japan's wage data surprises higher, BoJ hikes faster than priced (causing large SGD/JPY falls), while if Singapore GDP surprises higher, MAS may tighten slightly (causing modest SGD/JPY rises). This asymmetry of magnitude favours the bearish SGD/JPY direction strategically.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: SGD/JPY is a dual-safe-haven pair with non-symmetric risk properties. JPY is the textbook global safe-haven with the strongest negative VIX correlation in G10, while SGD is a regional Asian safe-haven. In acute global risk-off (VIX 30), JPY dominates because global carry trades are unwound, with SGD-funded positions in EM assets being liquidated and proceeds repatriated to JPY. In moderate risk aversion (VIX 20–30), Singapore benefits from regional capital flight from Hong Kong, ASEAN, and Gulf states — partially offsetting JPY safe-haven demand. At the current VIX of approximately 23–25 (elevated but not panic), the balance is marginally in JPY's favour because the Iran conflict has disproportionately impacted Japan's energy-dependent economy, creating additional selling pressure on JPY crosses, but the directional force of the safe-haven channel narrowly favours JPY over SGD on a bilateral basis. The Iran-driven energy shock is a direct negative for JPY via ToT but simultaneously drives risk aversion (JPY positive) — the net effect is highly uncertain, making this a contested factor. The SGD's position as Asia's financial safety deposit box attracts Gulf capital flows during the crisis, providing some bilateral support for SGD vs JPY even in risk-off.

Neither risk channel dominates strategically, making this factor neutral for the pair over 1–6 months. The tail risk of a sustained Hormuz closure remains the most negative scenario for SGD/JPY (both Japan energy costs and global recession risk fall), though it is also the scenario where JPY safe-haven demand would likely dominate SGD.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The tactical flow picture for SGD/JPY is decisively influenced by Japan's fiscal year-end on March 31 — a structurally documented seasonal pattern that is actively in play within the 2–4 week window. Japanese corporations, life insurers, and pension funds historically repatriate foreign income and settle FX obligations in the final 3–4 weeks of March, generating persistent yen buying across all JPY crosses including SGD/JPY. This fiscal year-end flow is the most actionable bilateral-specific flow dynamic in the pair over the immediate tactical window. Additionally, Japanese life insurer FX hedge ratios have fallen to 44.4–45.7% (a 14-year low), meaning any sudden JPY appreciation forces scrambled hedge adjustments that amplify the move non-linearly. For SGD, the MAS reserve accumulation story (+US$18bn in 5 months) is bullish SGD but represents slow-moving structural flows rather than tactical event-driven flow. The bilateral positioning implication: speculative JPY shorts (-34,225 CFTC leveraged contracts) are vulnerable to a tactical squeeze during year-end, while SGD's long structural bias provides modest support but cannot match the acute seasonal force of Japanese repatriation.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, yen carry trade positioning has grown increasingly fragile after several years of uninterrupted leverage accumulation; as a result, even modest shifts in expectations can trigger outsized moves as positions unwind. SGD/JPY is not a primary JPY carry trade destination (the +7bps bilateral carry is trivial) but the pair is indirectly exposed to the global carry unwind channel: as the BoJ hikes and narrows the USD/JPY differential, the global JPY carry trade structure (estimated $1–2 trillion notional) unwinds, strengthening JPY broadly including against SGD. The structural Singapore inflows (wealth management, semiconductor exports, MAS reserve accumulation) provide durable support for SGD, but the sheer scale of the potential JPY carry unwind — accelerated by Japanese life insurer re-hedging at 14-year low hedge ratios — creates a force that can overwhelm the incremental SGD structural bid during episodic carry unwinding events.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Net commodity exposure for SGD/JPY requires careful bilateral netting. For oil specifically: SGD is a net negative (oil importer) but partially offset by refining margin benefits (~$30/bbl) and Singapore's role as Asia's premier bunkering port. JPY is strongly negative for oil (87% energy import dependency, 70% of crude via Hormuz). Net bilateral effect of oil rising: mildly bearish SGD/JPY (JPY benefits more from oil normalisation; JPY suffers more from sustained high oil). The current Brent at ~$88 (down from $119.50 peak) is creating a complex bilateral dynamic: JPY's ToT headwind from oil reduces JPY fundamentally, but the safe-haven channel partially offsets (VIX elevated → JPY bid). Singapore's refining margins at ~$30/bbl are a modest positive for SGD's ToT that the market underestimates. The bilateral trade relationship is modest: Japan imports from Singapore ~US$19.82bn while Singapore imports from Japan ~US$21.19bn, representing a marginal bilateral trade deficit for Singapore that creates modest net JPY demand. However, this is dwarfed by Singapore's overall current account surplus of 18% of GDP. The current oil trajectory from $119 → $88 is a net negative for SGD/JPY — the de-escalation trade benefits JPY more than SGD on ToT grounds.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, if Brent normalises to $70–80 under the Iran de-escalation base case, Japan's external balance recovers substantially (oil bill falls by ¥5–8 trillion, boosting the current account surplus toward 5.5–6% of GDP). Singapore's external balance is already the strongest in G10+SGD at 18% of GDP and cannot structurally improve by a comparable magnitude. The strategic external balance trajectory therefore favours JPY relative to SGD — Japan's balance recovers from a temporarily depressed level while Singapore's is already at a structural peak. Japan's record primary income surplus (¥41.59 trillion) continues to generate repatriation pressure regardless of oil. The semiconductor cycle sustaining Singapore's NODX growth is a bullish SGD offset, but even a $975bn global semiconductor market provides only incremental improvement to a current account already at 18% of GDP. The bilateral edge is Japan's external balance recovery from the oil shock being larger than Singapore's incremental improvement.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: SGD: net oil importer but partially offset by refining margins (~$30/bbl) and Singapore's role as Asia's premier bunkering port; LNG re-export hub provides additional partial offset
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: JPY: strongly negative oil exposure — 87% primary energy import dependency, 90%+ crude from Middle East via Hormuz; no meaningful refining margin offset; most oil-import-sensitive currency in G10
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil rising: SGD impact = negative (importer) offset by refining margins → net neutral for SGD. Step 2 — Oil rising: JPY impact = strong negative (importer, no refining offset) → net bearish JPY. Step 3 — Net bilateral oil-up effect: SGD less damaged than JPY → bullish SGD/JPY when oil rises. Step 4 — Oil falling (de-escalation): SGD = modestly positive; JPY = strongly positive (ToT improvement). Step 5 — Net bilateral oil-down effect: JPY benefits more from oil normalisation than SGD → bearish SGD/JPY when oil falls. Step 6 — Current trajectory ($119 → $88, de-escalation): net negative for SGD/JPY as JPY ToT recovery is larger in magnitude than SGD's incremental improvement.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil to ~$97/bbl: SGD modestly negative (import costs rise, partially offset by refining margins); JPY strongly negative (no offset mechanism); net SGD/JPY mildly bullish (+50–100 pips) as SGD is less damaged than JPY on ToT basis alone, though safe-haven channel could amplify JPY demand in risk-off simultaneously — net effect ambiguous and direction-dependent on risk sentiment
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil to ~$79/bbl: SGD modestly positive (import costs fall); JPY strongly positive (large ToT improvement from already-elevated import costs — ¥5+ trillion annualised relief); net SGD/JPY bearish (-100–200 pips) as Japan's external balance recovery is disproportionately larger than Singapore's incremental improvement from an already-peak 18% of GDP surplus
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Brent crude oil is overwhelmingly the dominant commodity driver for SGD/JPY given Japan's extreme energy import dependency (87% primary energy) vs Singapore's partial refining margin offset. The direction of oil movement determines whether the ToT or safe-haven channel dominates for JPY, making crude the most complex external variable for this pair.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both central banks have credible frameworks but communicate very differently. MAS uses the semi-annual MPS to adjust S$NEER band parameters — the next meeting is April 14. There will be no MAS communication in the March 17–19 super week, meaning MAS credibility has no near-term tactical expression. The BoJ will communicate on March 18: a hold is 95% priced but the statement language and press conference will be scrutinised for signals regarding April. If Governor Ueda removes the Iran-cautious language from the February 26 communication and signals greater confidence, markets will price a higher probability of April hike — bearish SGD/JPY. If Ueda maintains caution, April probability falls — neutral to mildly bullish SGD/JPY. The bilateral credibility differential is modestly in MAS's favour (44-year track record vs BoJ's recent recovery from ultra-loose policy), but the active policy divergence creates an asymmetry: BoJ communication moves markets more than MAS communication because the BoJ is in active regime transition. This gives the BoJ's March 18 statement an outsized impact on SGD/JPY relative to MAS's silence.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the guidance divergence between MAS and BoJ creates a clear directional force for SGD/JPY. MAS's April review will either hold (maintaining current pace of appreciation) or tighten marginally (slope increase). The BoJ's April 27–28 meeting has approximately 60% probability of delivering a 25bp hike to 1.00% — the market is expecting two or three further policy rate hikes between December 2025 and the end of 2026, with the most likely scenario for long-term interest rates being a gradual increase. The bilateral sequencing matters: MAS acts April 14, BoJ acts April 27–28. If MAS holds and BoJ hikes at the subsequent meeting, the guidance divergence strongly favours JPY over the subsequent 6 months. Even if MAS tightens (slope increase), the MAS action is an implicit 2% per annum appreciation — not a rate hike that compresses differentials. The BoJ hike, by contrast, directly narrows the USD-JPY rate differential that has been the primary yen anchor. Strategically, the BoJ's guidance credibility — having already demonstrated willingness to hike from -0.10% to 0.75% in 23 months against prevailing consensus at each step — is underpriced relative to the SGD/JPY forward curve.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The fiscal differential is among the starkest in G10+SGD. Singapore: AAA rated by all three agencies, combined sovereign assets exceeding US$1.1 trillion, fiscal surplus in 2026 of approximately 0.4% of GDP, gross government debt issued for investment purposes not to fund deficits, and net asset position massively positive. Japan: A+/A1/A rated (eight notches below Singapore), gross debt/GDP of ~235% (highest in developed world), FY2026 budget at a record ¥122.3 trillion with defence buildup and fiscal expansion under PM Takaichi. Tactically this factor is unambiguously bullish SGD/JPY — the fiscal differential is one of the largest between any two G10+SGD pairs and cannot be contested. However, at the tactical 2–4 week horizon, fiscal dynamics move slowly and this factor functions as a floor rather than a near-term catalyst.

The bilateral fiscal factor is neutral strategically because the directions of influence roughly cancel — Singapore's fiscal strength is priced and stable, while Japan's fiscal expansion introduces uncertainty whose direction of impact on the pair is ambiguous (could accelerate or slow BoJ normalisation).

expression_assessment.directional_expression: Given the thin liquidity and wide spreads of SGD/JPY (3–5 pips normal, 8–12 pips during stress), the directional bearish view is best expressed through component pairs: (1) Short USD/JPY as the primary expression — liquid, captures BoJ normalisation with tight execution; (2) Short USD/SGD captures MAS appreciation. The combined position is equivalent to long SGD/JPY with far superior liquidity and execution efficiency. For a bearish SGD/JPY view, a direct short SGD/JPY spot position is viable but requires careful sizing given thin market depth. Alternatively, short USD/SGD funded by short USD/JPY creates a synthetic short SGD/JPY with better execution.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The March 17–19 super-central-bank week creates a short-dated vol spike that will decay rapidly after the event cluster. Entering directional positions before March 18 means paying elevated event premium in options. Spot positions are unaffected by vol decay but exposed to binary event risk. The April 27–28 BoJ meeting is the most critical near-term event for the strategic position — ideally the full strategic allocation would be established before that meeting given the asymmetric payoff if BoJ hikes to 1.00%.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: SGD/JPY is a thin cross with wider bid-ask spreads than G10 majors (estimated 3–5 pip spreads in normal conditions, widening to 8–12 pips during Asia stress events). Position sizing must account for reduced market depth relative to USD/JPY or USD/SGD. Limit order usage is strongly recommended over market orders. For larger position sizes, the synthetic approach via component pairs (short USD/JPY + short USD/SGD) provides significantly better execution quality and tighter effective spreads.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: SGD/JPY is constructed from two USD crosses, meaning USD liquidity conditions affect both legs simultaneously through the vehicle currency channel. When USD weakens broadly (DXY declining from 104+ to ~97.9), both USD/SGD and USD/JPY are affected — the net impact on SGD/JPY depends on the relative sensitivity. Historically, USD weakness is more bullish for SGD (managed appreciation strengthens) than it is bearish for JPY (which also strengthens in USD weakness but is constrained by carry trade dynamics). The Fed's end of QT (December 2025) and transition to Reserve Management Purchases represent a structural USD-easing event, but since both currencies benefit from USD weakness, the net SGD/JPY impact is muted. The cross-currency basis for SGD/JPY is not directly quoted but can be estimated through the component bases: SGD/USD basis and JPY/USD basis. Given Japan's fiscal year-end creates seasonal USD demand (slightly positive for USD/JPY basis widening), while Singapore's abundant reserves keep SGD/USD basis stable, the net SGD/JPY USD funding impact is marginally negative for SGD/JPY tactically — Japanese entities need to buy USD at year-end, which comes from selling JPY and potentially selling SGD-denominated assets.

The key USD variable for SGD/JPY is the dollar smile positioning: if the US enters mild recession (increasing probability given February NFP -92K), USD weakens significantly, potentially boosting both currencies further. The bilateral edge from this factor does not clearly favour either direction.

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.1275 on a -1 to +1 scale indicates a marginal bearish lean, insufficient for high conviction. The pair is effectively in a standoff between two bullish dossiers. The dominant negative contributors are Risk Appetite (-0.072) and Flows & Positioning (-0.060), with a partial offset from Fiscal & Sovereign (+0.032). The score maps to Conviction 2/5 — a slight directional bias but well within the noise range for any given 2-4 week window.
tactical.rationale: The tactical SGD/JPY picture is defined by a near-standoff between two independently bullish currency dossiers. The dominant bilateral factor at the 2–4 week horizon is the Japan fiscal year-end on March 31 (Factor 4, -0.060 contribution): well-documented seasonal repatriation flows create durable JPY demand across all JPY crosses, and this year's institutional underhedging (life insurer hedge ratios at 14-year lows) creates additional non-linear amplification risk. The risk appetite factor (-0.072) reflects that at the current VIX of 23–25, JPY's global safe-haven properties marginally outcompete SGD's regional safe-haven status in the bilateral relationship — though this could reverse rapidly on Iran ceasefire headlines. The fiscal supremacy of Singapore (+0.032) is a structural floor that prevents sustained SGD/JPY weakness beyond approximately ¥118–119. The interaction between the SGD and JPY dossiers is fundamentally conflicting: both standalone analyses are bullish their respective currencies, which mechanically produces a muted pair signal. The edge is in the timing asymmetry — the BoJ-positive catalysts (Shunto March 12, BoJ statement March 18, fiscal year-end March 31) are all concentrated within the tactical window, while the MAS-positive catalyst (April 14 review) falls OUTSIDE the tactical window. This sequencing tilts the tactical near-term flow in JPY's favour. The Iran conflict introduces binary tail risk: ceasefire would collapse JPY safe-haven bid and briefly push SGD/JPY toward ¥124–125 in a rapid short squeeze as carry trades resume — this scenario should be watched daily given the diplomatic signals that emerged on March 10. Volatility will remain elevated (8–10% IV) through the super-week, compressing thereafter. Conviction is limited to 2/5 because the score is only slightly negative — well within the noise range — and the binary Iran outcome could dominate all fundamental signals within 2 weeks. The FOMC on March 18 adds ambiguity through the vehicle currency channel, as the net SGD/JPY impact of Fed guidance depends on the relative sensitivity of each leg to USD moves. Both the carry channel (near-zero, +7bps) and the positioning channel (45th percentile CFTC — no extremes) provide no directional anchor, leaving event sequencing as the primary source of near-term directional bias.

Among JPY cross expressions for the bullish JPY thesis, SGD/JPY is less attractive than short USD/JPY or short EUR/JPY because the near-zero carry differential means no carry penalty for being in the wrong direction, but the thin liquidity and execution costs make it a secondary expression vehicle relative to more liquid JPY crosses.

strategic.rationale: The strategic SGD/JPY bear case is built on three interlocking pillars that reinforce rather than merely aggregate. First, Policy & Rates (0.24 weight, -0.156 contribution) is the dominant driver: the BoJ's systematic hiking toward 1.00–1.25% by year-end will invert the bilateral rate differential (currently +7bps SGD) to approximately -18 to -43bps (JPY earns more), marking the first carry reversal in this cycle and removing the structural rationale for long SGD/JPY positioning. Second, Guidance & Credibility (0.12 weight, -0.078 contribution) amplifies the policy signal: the BoJ has established a credible normalisation trajectory having raised rates from -0.10% to 0.75% in 23 months despite near-universal skepticism at each hike, while MAS's managed float operates through slope adjustments that are categorically smaller in magnitude than a sequence of BoJ rate hikes. Third, External Balance (0.16 weight, -0.096 contribution) provides the structural medium-term anchor: Japan's record primary income surplus of ¥41.59 trillion creates persistent repatriation flows, and the oil shock recovery (Brent normalising from $119 to $70–80 under the base case) will deliver a larger relative boost to Japan's external balance than to Singapore's already-peak 18% of GDP surplus. The interaction between the two dossiers reveals a critical asymmetry that the standalone analyses obscure: SGD's bullish case is already delivered (exchange rate near 11-year high, BIS REER at 92nd percentile, semiconductor cycle well-advanced), while JPY's bullish case is just beginning to materialise (REER at all-time low, 0th percentile, hiking cycle in early innings). This timing divergence — SGD has already repriced while JPY's revaluation is prospective — creates the edge for the bearish SGD/JPY view. The Inflation & Growth factor (-0.055 contribution) reinforces the asymmetry: Japan's wage-price cycle entering self-sustainability for the first time since the 1990s has a larger, more direct policy transmission impact than Singapore's semiconductor export acceleration. The path of least resistance for the pair is lower as BoJ hikes compress the differential toward zero/negative while MAS maintains but does not dramatically accelerate its appreciation slope. Conviction is 3/5 rather than 4/5 because Singapore's structural surplus and managed float provide a durable floor that prevents the pair falling beyond ¥108–112 under any realistic scenario, and because the semiconductor supercycle gives SGD a genuine fundamental tailwind that limits the downside magnitude and introduces timing uncertainty about when exactly the BoJ-driven move will materialise.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: SGD/JPY bearish ranks as moderately attractive among SGD crosses and JPY crosses at the strategic horizon. Among JPY appreciation expressions, long JPY via SGD/JPY is less efficient than short USD/JPY (better liquidity, larger differential) but more efficient than short AUD/JPY or GBP/JPY (those pairs carry 310bp and 300bp negative carry respectively, while SGD/JPY is near-zero carry; the SGD/JPY bearish expression has minimal carry cost). Among SGD bearish expressions (if one wanted to fade SGD), long USD/SGD remains the cleanest (most liquid, MAS-managed). SGD/JPY serves as a niche but intellectually compelling relative value expression of the BoJ normalisation theme with near-zero carry cost.

conditions: BoJ hikes to 1.00% at the April or June meeting
Fed cuts 25–50bps by September 2026; Iran conflict de-escalates by end-Q2 with Brent normalising to $70–80
Shunto delivers 5.3–5.8% validating BoJ's wage-price cycle thesis
MAS holds at April review with maintained appreciation slope
base_case.path: Phase 1 (March–April): Consolidation at ¥119–123 as March event cluster clears; pair tests ¥121 support on fiscal year-end flows. Phase 2 (April–June): BoJ hike at April 27–28 to 1.00% breaks pair decisively below ¥120, targeting ¥117–118 (200-DMA region) rapidly given thin liquidity. Phase 3 (June–September): Sustained pressure toward ¥113–116 as bilateral carry turns negative and institutional rehedging by Japanese life insurers provides structural flow support for JPY.

conditions: BoJ delays all hikes beyond June 2026 (Takaichi political pressure slows normalisation)
MAS tightens slope at April review AND semiconductor cycle continues at 50%+ electronics NODX growth through Q2
BoJ hikes twice (April + June) to 1.25%; US enters mild recession (Fed cuts 75bps)
Japanese life insurer forced re-hedging creates a non-linear JPY buying cascade; Iran sustained conflict creates a risk-off regime favouring JPY over SGD

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path unfolds in three phases. Phase 1 (March–April): Consolidation at ¥119–123 as the March event cluster clears; the pair tests support at ¥121 (50-DMA) on Japanese fiscal year-end flows but recovers modestly afterward as the acute seasonal flow subsides. Phase 2 (April–June): If BoJ hikes at the April 27–28 meeting to 1.00%, SGD/JPY breaks decisively below ¥120 and targets ¥117–118 (200-DMA region); this move could be rapid (200–300 pips in days) given thin cross-pair liquidity and the structural significance of the first carry inversion in this cycle. Phase 3 (June–September): Sustained pressure toward ¥113–116 as the bilateral carry turns negative (BoJ at 1.00+% vs SORA at 0.82%) and institutional rehedging by Japanese life insurers provides structural flow support. Pullbacks toward ¥120–122 should be treated as entry points for the strategic bearish view rather than signals of trend reversal.

increase_conditions: BoJ hikes in both April and June (reaching 1.25%); bilateral carry turns negative by 40+bps; Japanese life insurer re-hedging visibly accelerates — increase to 5/5
BoJ hikes in April to 1.00% AND SGD/JPY breaks below ¥120 on sustained volume — increase to 4/5
decrease_conditions: BoJ delays all hikes beyond September 2026 AND MAS tightens slope at April review (SGD acceleration outpaces JPY normalisation) — decrease to 2/5
BoJ enters explicit pause AND semiconductor cycle accelerates to 70%+ electronics NODX growth (SGD strengthening faster AND JPY normalisation stalling simultaneously) — decrease to 1/5

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: -650 JPY pips / -5.3% (SGD/JPY from 122.00 to 115.50; bearish position profits)
risk_reward.downside_case_loss: -1,350 pips / -11.1% (SGD/JPY from 122.00 to 108.50; downside scenario is also bearish SGD/JPY — profits further)
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 17.7:1 probability-adjusted (downside scenario also profits for bearish position)
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Highly favourable. The upside scenario (SGD/JPY rising materially) requires multiple simultaneous conditions that conflict with the fundamental case. The distribution of outcomes is skewed dramatically bearish SGD/JPY because even the 'adverse' downside scenario has SGD/JPY falling further — the bear case is only genuinely threatened by the base-case upside scenario where the pair holds at ¥118–125.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Early-2026 bank forecasts from Credit Agricole, DBS, ING, and Westpac center around ¥116–117 by end-2026, while MUFG and DBS introduce downside skew toward ¥112–114 by year-end. Central consensus: ~¥116–117 (approximately 3.5–4.9% decline from current ¥122).
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Neutral with slight bearish bias (net score -0.1275); SGD/JPY target ¥119–121 over 2–4 weeks
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Bearish SGD/JPY (conviction 3/5); target ¥113–118 over 3–6 months with skew toward the lower end at ¥113–115
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: broadly in-line directionally; skewed below consensus on downside probability
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Directionally aligned at ¥116–117 central target; our skew is toward ¥113–115 with higher probability assigned to the accelerated downside scenario than bank consensus allows
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We weight the carry inversion scenario more heavily than bank models — if BoJ reaches 1.00% by June/July, the bilateral carry flips negative and could accelerate declines toward ¥110–113 more rapidly than consensus projects. We also believe the institutional underhedging at Japanese life insurers (44.4–45.7% hedge ratios, 14-year low) represents a non-linear risk that consensus smooths over — a sudden JPY appreciation triggered by the April BoJ hike could produce a cascade of forced hedging adjustments that compresses SGD/JPY by 500+ pips in days, not weeks. Furthermore, we believe the upside scenario (SGD/JPY holding above ¥120) requires more simultaneous conditions than consensus assigns probability to — specifically BoJ disappointment on growth, wages, AND policy pace failing concurrently.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Short SGD/JPY from 122.00 to 115.00 = 700 pips profit, approximately 5.7% return. If the accelerated scenario materialises (SGD/JPY to ¥110), 1,200 pips = 9.8% return.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If SGD/JPY remains at ¥122–125 through September, the cost is near-zero carry (only +7bps annually) plus bid-ask spread costs in a thin market — approximately 50–100 pips of implicit cost over 6 months, making the cost of being wrong unusually low for this pair.

cross_horizon_consistency.tactical_stance: Neutral (slight bearish bias, -0.1275 net score, conviction 2/5)
cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: The tactical and strategic stances are aligned directionally (both lean bearish SGD/JPY) but differ significantly in conviction: 2/5 tactical vs 3/5 strategic. This divergence is mechanistically justified — the tactical window is dominated by binary event risk (Iran, Shunto, central bank cluster) that could push the pair in either direction by 200–300 pips on any given day, while the strategic horizon allows the fundamental policy convergence thesis to compound with greater certainty as multiple BoJ hike decisions confirm the normalisation path.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: April 27–28 BoJ meeting: if the BoJ hikes to 1.00%, tactical conviction immediately jumps to 4/5 because the carry inversion thesis crystallises and the strategic and tactical stances converge at high conviction.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Begin tactical position at modest size (50% of strategic allocation), using the March 17–19 event cluster as an anchor for adding. If SGD/JPY spikes to ¥124–125 on Iran ceasefire (upside case), add to the short at better levels — this is the preferred entry point. Scale to full strategic allocation only after BoJ April meeting confirms the hiking direction. The near-zero carry means the cost of holding through the volatile tactical window is minimal in interest rate terms, though execution costs in the thin SGD/JPY market demand careful limit order usage.

forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: 415 pips SGD/JPY lower than forward-implied (market prices flat; we expect ~118.00)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: 730 pips SGD/JPY lower than forward-implied (market prices ~122.30; we expect ~115.00)

catalysts.rationale: Rengo demand at 5.85–5.94% versus 2025 settlement of 5.25%. A result above 5.5% directly validates the BoJ's inflation sustainability assessment and pulls forward April hike probability above 70–75%. This is the single most important near-term catalyst for SGD/JPY — stronger Shunto results create immediate JPY buying across all crosses including SGD/JPY, while also reinforcing the strategic carry inversion thesis as BoJ moves faster toward 1.00%. The bilateral transmission is direct: higher Japanese wages → BoJ hikes sooner → bilateral rate differential compresses/inverts → SGD/JPY falls.

January electronics NODX at +56.1% YoY set an extraordinary benchmark. A February print confirming electronics export momentum above +30% would reinforce the semiconductor cycle narrative, lift MAS April tightening expectations toward 55%+, and provide a tactical SGD/JPY lift in the 24 hours following release. This is the primary SGD-positive near-term catalyst and is scheduled just one day before the BoJ decision, creating a potential binary 48-hour window of competing signals — strong Singapore NODX (SGD/JPY up) followed by hawkish BoJ statement (SGD/JPY down).

Hold is 95% priced, making the statement language the sole source of directional information for the market. If Governor Ueda removes or softens the Iran-cautious language from the February 26 communication, the market will interpret this as an implicit green light for an April hike, immediately pricing SGD/JPY 150–200 pips lower. Even a hawkish hold with unchanged language is mildly JPY-positive at these levels as it signals continued normalisation intent. The bilateral impact is asymmetric: a dovish surprise (explicit delay language) would push SGD/JPY toward ¥124, while a hawkish surprise (removal of caution language) would push toward ¥119–120.

A dovish hold with dot plot showing 2+ cuts would weaken USD broadly, boosting both SGD and JPY through the vehicle currency channel. The bilateral net effect on SGD/JPY depends on relative sensitivity: historically USD weakness is slightly more JPY-positive (carry unwind dynamics) than SGD-positive (managed float moderates the transmission), suggesting a mildly bearish SGD/JPY outcome from a dovish FOMC. A hawkish surprise (1 cut or hold) would modestly support the dollar against both currencies simultaneously — net ambiguous for the cross. The FOMC occurs the same day as the BoJ, making the 24-hour window of March 18 the highest bilateral event risk density of the entire tactical window.

The Iran oil spike (Brent from ~$65 to $119 and now $88) will push Singapore headline CPI toward 1.8–2.0% in February data. If MAS Core Inflation rises from 1.0% toward 1.5–2.0%, the market will significantly reprice April MAS tightening odds from 47% toward 65%+, providing a sharp SGD/JPY boost of 100–200 pips. This would temporarily overwhelm the BoJ normalisation headwind for the pair, creating a potential tactical entry point for the strategic bearish position at better levels near ¥123–124. The bilateral importance is that this is the only MAS-positive catalyst in the tactical window that can produce an immediate and measurable SGD/JPY spike.

Japan's documented seasonal FX pattern of corporate repatriation, life insurer FX book closure, and pension fund settlement generates consistent yen demand in the final 7–10 trading days of March. This year's episode is amplified by institutional underhedging at 14-year lows (hedge ratios at 44.4–45.7%). SGD/JPY is directly in the path of this flow — Japanese institutions repatriating proceeds from Singapore-denominated assets or closing SGD/JPY synthetic positions create selling pressure on the pair in the ¥120–122 zone. The bilateral importance is specific: Japan is one of Singapore's largest FDI sources, meaning Japanese institutional holdings of SGD-denominated assets are material and their March year-end settlement creates a direct SGD/JPY flow.

A 47% probability of MAS slope increase represents a near-coin-flip binary event with significant market impact. If MAS tightens, SGD appreciates sharply across all crosses, pushing SGD/JPY back toward ¥123–125 with a 200–300 pip immediate response. This is the most important single SGD-positive catalyst in the 6-month window and falls 13 days before the BoJ April meeting. Any MAS-driven SGD/JPY spike should be viewed as a strategic entry point for the bearish position before the BoJ April 27–28 meeting provides the offsetting JPY catalyst. A MAS hold (53% probability) would be mildly SGD/JPY negative as it signals the managed float path is unchanged — providing no incremental SGD support against the building BoJ hike thesis.

catalysts.event: BoJ April Meeting with Outlook Report
catalysts.rationale: Approximately 60% probability of a 25bp hike to 1.00% — the highest BoJ rate since 1995, representing a landmark in the normalisation cycle. A hike to 1.00% with SORA at 0.82% flips the bilateral carry differential negative (-18bps), marking the first carry inversion in the cycle. The immediate market reaction in SGD/JPY would be 300–500 pips lower as carry trades are unwound and institutional re-hedging accelerates. This is the single most powerful directional catalyst for the strategic bearish SGD/JPY view, and the Outlook Report accompanying the decision would provide the BoJ's explicit rate path signal through 2026. The bilateral carry inversion at this meeting transforms the pair from a pure-direction trade to one with a structural carry headwind for longs.

catalysts.event: Japan Life Insurer Annual Portfolio Rebalancing (FY2026 H1 announcement)
catalysts.rationale: Following fiscal year-end, Japanese life insurers announce their annual investment plans for FY2026. With hedge ratios already at 14-year lows (44.4–45.7%), any signal of hedge ratio normalisation toward 50%+ would represent hundreds of billions of yen in FX buying demand across all JPY crosses. Singapore-dollar-denominated assets held by Japanese institutions (bonds, equities, real estate via J-REITs with Singapore exposure) would be a source of potential cover, directly impacting SGD/JPY flows. This is the non-linear institutional catalyst that consensus models smooth over — a sudden announcement of hedge ratio targets would front-load the entire year's worth of repatriation pressure.

catalysts.event: BoJ June Meeting AND FOMC June SEP (Concurrent)
catalysts.rationale: If BoJ did not hike in April (40% scenario), June becomes the high-conviction hike meeting. The simultaneous FOMC June meeting with Summary of Economic Projections could deliver both a BoJ hike AND Fed cut/dovish guidance in the same week — the most powerful imaginable bilateral policy catalyst for SGD/JPY downside. The combination of BoJ hike + Fed cut compresses the USD-JPY differential by 50bps in one week, triggering both carry trade unwinding (JPY positive across all crosses) and dollar weakness (incremental SGD positive, but smaller effect). The net bilateral impact would likely be 400–700 pips of SGD/JPY downside within the week, with acceleration through the ¥118 (200-DMA) support level triggering CTA momentum selling.

The 10% US tariff on Singapore exports (semiconductor-exempt) expires after 150 days. Removal eliminates a minor headwind to Singapore's export competitiveness and reduces trade policy uncertainty for the second half of 2026. The direct impact on SGD/JPY is modest (~20–30 pips) but the confidence boost to Singapore growth outlook could support MAS's decision to maintain or further tighten the appreciation slope in the October review, providing a small but visible SGD/JPY positive in the weeks around the expiry. The bilateral importance is that this confirmation of US-Singapore trade normalisation (contrasting with the US-Japan trade framework agreed July 2025) removes a tail risk for the SGD leg of the pair.

invalidators.condition: BoJ delivers an explicit pause statement at the March 18 meeting, citing Iran conflict uncertainty and explicitly delaying any April action commitment with 'extended monitoring period' language or equivalent
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish SGD/JPY (pair rising) — tactical view reverses; strategic conviction falls to 2/5
invalidators.mechanism: The dominant bearish driver — BoJ normalisation pace — would stall, making the MAS appreciation slope the dominant directional force. Without a credible BoJ hiking path, the bilateral rate differential remains +7bps (SGD positive) and the carry inversion thesis evaporates, removing the strategic bear case's primary pillar.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BoJ March 18 statement language — watch for 'extended monitoring period,' 'heightened uncertainty,' or any explicit reference to delaying policy normalisation in response to geopolitical conditions

invalidators.condition: MAS tightens the S$NEER slope at the April 14 review (explicit slope increase communicated in the Monetary Policy Statement)
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral/Bullish SGD/JPY for 2–4 weeks following the decision, before strategic bearish view reasserts ahead of BoJ April 27–28
invalidators.mechanism: The immediate MAS tightening impact on SGD (200–300 pip SGD/JPY spike) would dominate over the 2–4 weeks following the April 14 decision. However, if BoJ hikes 13 days later at the April 27–28 meeting, the MAS-driven SGD/JPY spike becomes an attractive entry point for the strategic bearish position. The key question is whether both actions occur in sequence (MAS tighten April 14 followed by BoJ hike April 27) — this scenario is actually most favourable for the bearish SGD/JPY view as it provides better entry.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: MAS April 14 Monetary Policy Statement — any change to 'modest and gradual' appreciation language or explicit band parameter adjustment signal

invalidators.condition: Shunto 2026 wage results disappoint below 5.0% (below the 2025 settlement of 5.25%)
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish SGD/JPY (pair rising) — tactical view reverses to bullish; strategic conviction falls to 2/5
invalidators.mechanism: The primary domestic justification for BoJ April hike evaporates if Shunto disappoints. April hike probability would fall from ~60% to <30%, triggering broad JPY selling as the normalisation timeline extends. Without the wage-price cycle confirmation, the BoJ has no mandate to accelerate hikes, the carry inversion scenario delays by 6–12 months, and the pair could rally toward ¥125–127 on JPY short-covering.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Rengo announcement of Shunto 2026 concentrated response day results (~March 12); subsequent monthly labour cash earnings from Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare

invalidators.condition: SGD/JPY closes above ¥124.00 on a weekly basis on sustained volume (not a single intraday spike)
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral — bearish view paused pending reassessment
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained weekly close above ¥124.00 would constitute a technical breakout above the 2026 range high at ¥122–123, confirming bullish technical momentum that would override the fundamental bearish case in the near term. Trend-following CTAs would flip to long SGD/JPY, and the double-top formation would be invalidated. The strategic bearish view would need to be reassessed with stops moved higher to ¥126.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SGD/JPY weekly close vs ¥124.00 — TradingView SGD/JPY weekly chart; verify on sustained volume not Iran ceasefire spike

invalidators.condition: Japan's current account surplus falls below ¥25 trillion annualised (from ¥31.88 trillion in 2025), driven by Brent crude sustained above $100/bbl for 8+ consecutive weeks
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish SGD/JPY — strategic external balance factor flips from bearish to bullish
invalidators.mechanism: Singapore's 18% of GDP surplus would then be 2–3x larger than Japan's on a comparative basis, fundamentally altering the bilateral external balance argument. Japan's terms-of-trade would deteriorate to levels where the repatriation flow story reverses — Japan would need to sell foreign assets to fund the current account deficit rather than repatriating income surpluses. The oil-driven external balance deterioration at sustained $100+ Brent overwhelms the safe-haven channel and creates genuine structural JPY weakness.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Japan monthly current account data (MoF release, typically 6–8 weeks lag); Brent crude front-month futures continuous

invalidators.condition: Singapore electronics NODX turns negative on a 3-month rolling basis (3-month average falls below 0% YoY from current +56.1%)
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral — strategic conviction falls to 2/5 as SGD structural case weakens
invalidators.mechanism: The primary growth driver for SGD appreciation (semiconductor supercycle) would be signalling peak, removing the structural justification for MAS maintaining or tightening the appreciation slope. A negative electronics NODX would trigger downward revisions to Singapore GDP forecasts, potentially leading MAS to hold or even ease the S$NEER slope — shifting the pair's bilateral dynamic from a two-bullish-dossier standoff to a potentially more straightforward JPY appreciation story. The strategic conviction would fall as SGD's fundamentals deteriorate.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: IE Singapore monthly NODX release (electronics sub-component); WSTS quarterly semiconductor forecast updates

signposts.indicator: BoJ-MAS Rate Differential (Bilateral SORA vs BoJ policy rate)
signposts.confirms_if: Differential moves to 0bps or negative as BoJ hikes toward 1.00% while SORA remains below 1.00% — confirms carry inversion thesis and the primary strategic bearish driver
signposts.invalidates_if: Differential widens above +30bps (e.g., MAS introduces explicit rate instrument and raises SORA above 1.00%), confirming SGD carry advantage and removing the carry inversion catalyst
signposts.bilateral_importance: This indicator is unique to SGD/JPY — no other JPY cross has near-zero bilateral differential. The indicator is updated only at BoJ and MAS meetings, making each central bank decision a binary signpost event. When this differential inverts, it marks a structural regime shift for the pair with no carry compensation for SGD/JPY longs — an event that has no precedent in this cycle and would likely trigger systematic position unwinding.
signposts.frequency: Updated at each BoJ/MAS meeting (BoJ approximately 8x per year; MAS 2x per year)

signposts.current_level: ~199bps (SGS-JGB approximately -18bps; JPY positive at the long end)
signposts.confirms_if: UST-JGB narrows below 175bps (BoJ hiking, USD rates falling — JPY strengthens broadly including vs SGD, with SGD/JPY following lower)
signposts.invalidates_if: UST-JGB widens above 250bps (Fed holds hawkish, BoJ pauses — JPY carry resumes, SGD/JPY likely rises toward ¥125+)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The UST-JGB spread is the primary driver of USD/JPY and indirectly of SGD/JPY. When UST-JGB narrows, both USD/JPY and SGD/JPY tend to fall as JPY is repatriated and carry trades are unwound. Monitoring this spread daily provides early warning of the SGD/JPY trajectory with a typical 1–5 day lead time. For SGD/JPY specifically, the SGS-JGB long-end spread at -18bps already signals Japan's bond market pays more than Singapore's, creating a structural repatriation incentive that this spread monitors in real time.

signposts.indicator: Japan Labour Cash Earnings / Shunto Annual Wage Settlement
signposts.current_level: +3.0% YoY (Jan 2026 base pay); Shunto 2026 Rengo demand: 5.85–5.94% (result pending March 12)
signposts.confirms_if: Shunto result exceeds 5.5%; monthly labour cash earnings remain above 2.5% YoY — confirms BoJ wage-price cycle validation and faster hiking timeline
signposts.invalidates_if: Shunto disappoints below 5.0%; cash earnings slow below 2.0% YoY — removes BoJ's primary justification for April hike
signposts.bilateral_importance: Japan's wage data is uniquely important for SGD/JPY because it directly influences BoJ hiking speed, which determines whether the bilateral carry inverts. No other cross-pair involving SGD has a single domestic data point with such decisive bilateral impact. The Shunto annual settlement is a once-per-year event that sets the entire BoJ hiking trajectory for the following 12 months, making the March 12 result the highest-information data release for the strategic bearish SGD/JPY thesis.

signposts.confirms_if: Brent falls below $80/bbl — Japan's ToT recovers more than Singapore's, boosting JPY relative to SGD and confirming the strategic external balance bearish SGD/JPY thesis
signposts.invalidates_if: Brent sustains above $110/bbl for 8+ weeks — Japan's ToT collapse at this level overwhelms the safe-haven channel for JPY; both currencies negatively affected but JPY more so, structurally weakening the external balance argument for JPY and pushing SGD/JPY higher
signposts.bilateral_importance: Oil is uniquely bilateral for this pair because Japan is massively more oil-import-dependent than Singapore (87% vs Singapore's partial refining offset), yet both are net oil importers. Oil's direction determines whether the terms-of-trade or safe-haven channel dominates for JPY — and the net bilateral effect on SGD/JPY changes sign depending on whether oil is rising or falling. This makes Brent crude the most complex external variable for the pair and the one requiring daily monitoring as the Iran conflict evolves.

signposts.indicator: VIX / Japan Fiscal Year-End Repatriation Window
signposts.current_level: VIX ~23–25 (peaked 35.0 on March 9); Japan fiscal year-end: 20 trading days away (March 31)
signposts.confirms_if: VIX holds above 20 through March 31 (maintains safe-haven JPY premium) AND fiscal year-end flows visibly strengthen yen across all crosses in the final 2 weeks of March — confirms both the tactical risk-appetite bearish factor and the seasonal flow bearish factor simultaneously
signposts.invalidates_if: VIX collapses below 15 (carry trades fully resume; safe-haven bid evaporates) before fiscal year-end is complete — SGD/JPY likely reverses higher on risk appetite recovery overwhelming the seasonal repatriation flow
signposts.bilateral_importance: The VIX-SGD/JPY relationship is unique because both currencies are safe-havens but with different global vs regional beta. During the fiscal year-end window specifically, the seasonal yen repatriation flow creates a temporary but powerful SGD/JPY headwind that is specific to this pair's bilateral dynamics — Japan is a significant holder of SGD-denominated assets through its position as one of Singapore's largest FDI sources, meaning Japanese fiscal year-end flows have a direct SGD/JPY component beyond general JPY cross effects.
signposts.source: https://www.investing.com/indices/volatility-s-p-500-historical-data; MoF Japan monthly current account data

assumptions: Spot rate construction: SGD/JPY spot of 122.00 is derived from USD/SGD 1.2710 and USD/JPY 157.63. Note that exact computation (157.63 / 1.2710 = 124.02) produces a slightly different level than the 122.00 stated in the SGD dossier — we use 122.00 as stated in the SGD dossier as the bilateral reference rate, which may reflect marginally different exact spot rates than the rounded USD leg figures quoted.

SGD/JPY IV estimation: No liquid SGD/JPY options market data was accessible during research. The pair's implied volatility is estimated at 8–10% using the cross-pair volatility formula √(IV_USDSGD² + IV_USDJPY² − 2×ρ×IV_USDSGD×IV_USDJPY) where ρ (SGD/JPY correlation through USD) is estimated at ~0.3. This produces approximately 9% which is consistent with the dossier data for each leg (USD/SGD IV ~5.5%, USD/JPY IV ~10%).

Forward points for SGD/JPY are estimated from the near-zero bilateral interest rate differential (+7bps). No direct SGD/JPY forward market data was sourced — the estimated forward premium of roughly flat to +3 pips at 6 months is a model-derived estimate consistent with uncovered interest parity at near-zero differentials.

Bilateral trade data sourced from 2023–2024 data: Singapore imports from Japan ~US$21.19bn (2023); Japan exports to Singapore ~US$19.82bn (2024). Minor year-to-year variation may affect the exact bilateral trade balance figure, though the qualitative conclusion (balanced, modest net JPY demand from trade) is robust.

Institutional SGD/JPY bank forecasts (Credit Agricole, DBS, ING, Westpac at ¥116–117; MUFG and DBS at ¥112–114) were produced before the Iran conflict which began February 28, 2026, and before the January 2026 MAS review. They likely understate the current uncertainty and complexity of the bilateral analysis.

Weight adjustment methodology: Factor 3 (Risk Appetite) boosted from 0.10 to 0.12 tactical and 0.05 to 0.06 strategic because SGD/JPY is a uniquely bilateral safe-haven cross. Factor 10 (USD Liquidity) reduced from 0.11 to 0.09 tactical and 0.12 to 0.10 strategic because USD conditions affect both legs approximately equally for this non-USD cross. Factor 1 received the net redistribution of +0.02 from Factor 10. All other factors retain default weights. Total weights sum to 1.00 at both horizons.

CFTC positioning data for SGD is not available (SGD is not a reportable currency at the CFTC). SGD positioning inferences are derived from MAS reserve accumulation data (+US$18bn over 5 months) and qualitative flow analysis rather than direct speculative position data.

The tactical weighted score table in the markdown showed Factor 3 at -0.5 stance with -0.036 contribution (weight 0.12), and Factor 7 at -0.5 with -0.014 contribution (weight 0.05). This JSON reflects the full -1 stance scores for Factors 3 and 7 as stated in the factor sections (Bearish stance = -1), which produces slightly different weighted contributions than the markdown summary table's -0.5 stance scores.
